title: One School, One Nurse Act of 2025
short_title: One School, One Nurse Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill tells the Department of Education to give money to school districts so they can hire more school nurses. The goal is to make sure every elementary and secondary school has at least one full-time nurse. School districts that don't have enough nurses can apply for this money, and districts that really need help will get priority. The bill also says the Department of Education must create rules about how many nurses each school should have based on how many students go there.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Education and Workforce, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the One School, One Nurse Act of twenty twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Purpose; Findings
sections.text: The goal of this law is to help states and local school districts make sure every elementary and secondary school has at least one full-time registered nurse working there. It also aims to keep the right number of nurses compared to students by helping schools recruit, hire, and keep registered nurses. Congress has learned several important facts. The American Academy of Pediatrics says every elementary and secondary school should have at least one full-time registered nurse on staff. During the two thousand fifteen to two thousand sixteen school year, only about half of all public schools had a full-time registered nurse and nearly twenty percent did not have any nurse at all. Schools where more students qualified for free or reduced-price lunch had fewer school nurses. In two thousand nineteen, about six percent of children in the United States did not have health insurance. For children without health care coverage, a school nurse is often the main source of health services. Research shows that schools with a full-time registered nurse see many benefits including fewer student absences, students spending more time in class, more accurate medical records, higher vaccination rates, fewer student pregnancies, and better health outcomes for students with asthma or diabetes. School nurses play an important role in helping manage students' ongoing physical, emotional, mental, and social health needs. They conduct health screenings, help with vaccinations and making sure students meet vaccination requirements, reduce the workload on teachers and other school staff, and support a positive and healthy school environment.

sections.title: Section 3 One School, One Nurse Grant Program
sections.text: The Secretary of Education must create a program within twelve months of this law being passed. This program will give grants on a competitive basis to qualified organizations to carry out specific activities. These grants will last for five years. Any qualified organization that wants a grant must submit an application to the Secretary at the time, in the way, and with the information the Secretary requires. The application must include a needs assessment that shows there are ongoing shortages of school nurses or ongoing shortages of full-time school nurses. It must describe the health and wellness needs of the students served by the organization. The application must include a detailed plan for how grant money will be used to address the nurse shortages described in the needs assessment. This plan must explain how the activities will address the health and wellness needs and how the money will be used to make sure the organization continues to employ and keep school nurses after the grant period ends. The application must describe how the organization will focus on recruiting people from the communities it serves and from groups that are not well represented in public health jobs. It must explain how the organization will track progress in meeting any specific hiring goals. When giving out grants, the Secretary will give priority to organizations that are high-need local school districts or partnerships between state education agencies and groups of high-need local school districts. Priority also goes to organizations that describe and set specific hiring goals to increase the number of school nurses who are from groups not well represented in public health jobs. Grant money must be used for one or more of these activities: recruit and hire school nurses, provide resources needed to change part-time school nurse jobs at elementary or secondary schools into full-time jobs, support keeping school nurses by doing things like increasing salaries, and maintain the recommended number of nurses compared to students. Each organization that gets a grant must submit a yearly report to the Secretary for each year of the grant. The report must include a summary of the organization's progress in employing at least one full-time registered nurse at each elementary and secondary school it serves and maintaining the recommended number of nurses compared to students. It must include data on the number and percentage of full-time and part-time school nurses broken down by major racial and ethnic groups and gender at each school. The report must summarize any progress made in addressing the health and wellness needs identified in the needs assessment as a result of activities done with grant money. Within twelve months of this law being passed, the Secretary must work with the Secretary of Health and Human Services to create regulations that specify the recommended number of nurses compared to students for elementary and secondary schools. The Secretary must provide guidance and other technical help to organizations to achieve and maintain these ratios. The regulations must define what groups are not well represented in public health jobs for this program. The Secretary must also work with the Secretary of Health and Human Services and the Secretary of Labor to define what full-time means for school nurses in this program. For this section, qualified organization means a local school district or a partnership between a state education agency and a group of local school districts in the state created for the purpose of getting a grant under this program. High-need local school district has the same meaning as in section two hundred of the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty-five. Local school district and state education agency have the same meanings as in section eighty-one oh one of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of nineteen sixty-five. School nurse means a registered nurse who is employed by a school or local school district or state or state education agency and is qualified under state law to provide assessment, diagnosis, counseling, educational, therapeutic, and other health services to meet student needs. Secretary means the Secretary of Education.
